name: Balarama
description: Balarama, also known as Baladeva, is a significant deity in Hinduism, revered as an avatar of Vishnu. He is particularly associated with strength, agriculture, and righteous anger. Often depicted with fair skin and clad in blue garments, Balarama is the elder brother of Krishna and plays a crucial role in the narratives surrounding Krishna's life. He is considered the patron deity of farmers and is strongly linked to the plough, his signature weapon and symbol of agricultural prosperity. Balarama embodies the virtues of physical prowess and unwavering loyalty. While Krishna is celebrated for his wisdom and diplomacy, Balarama represents brute force and direct action. His stories often highlight his immense strength and his dedication to dharma, even when it clashes with the more strategic approaches of his younger brother. He is also sometimes considered an avatar of Shesha, the serpent on which Vishnu reclines.
subtitle: The Strong One

keyMyths.title: Balarama's Birth
keyMyths.description: Balarama's birth is marked by a miraculous transfer. Initially, he was conceived in the womb of Devaki, Krishna's mother. However, to protect him from Kamsa's murderous intentions, Vishnu commanded Yoga-Maya to transfer the embryo to the womb of Rohini, another wife of Vasudeva. This event led to his name 'Sankarshana,' meaning 'transferred'. This divine intervention ensured Balarama's survival and set the stage for his crucial role in the events to come.

keyMyths.title: Balarama's Anger and the River Yamuna
keyMyths.description: Once, Balarama desired to play in the river Yamuna, but the river refused to come closer to him. Enraged by this defiance, Balarama used his plough to divert the course of the river. He threatened to drag the Yamuna into the sea, demonstrating his immense power and unwavering will. The river, humbled by his strength, immediately complied with his wishes, highlighting Balarama's formidable nature and control over the natural world.

keyMyths.title: Balarama's Neutrality in the Kurukshetra War
keyMyths.description: During the Kurukshetra War, Balarama remained neutral despite his close relationships with both the Pandavas and the Kauravas. He was a disciple of Duryodhana in the art of mace fighting and felt obligated to avoid taking sides. This neutrality underscores his commitment to dharma and his refusal to compromise his principles, even in the face of a devastating war. He instead went on a pilgrimage during the war, showcasing his detachment from the conflict.

keyMyths.title: Balarama's Departure
keyMyths.description: Balarama ended his earthly existence by sitting in a meditative posture under a tree. A white serpent emerged from his mouth, representing his true form as Shesha, the serpent upon whom Vishnu rests. This departure signifies his divine origins and his return to his original form, marking the end of his avataric role and solidifying his connection to Vishnu. It is a peaceful and dignified exit befitting a deity of his stature.
